preFix.title: Making a leather phone sleeve
preFix.subtitle: Cut, punch, and stitch a simple two-panel leather sleeve for a phone or small tablet
preFix.excerpt: How to cut two matching panels of veg-tan leather for a phone sleeve, bevel and burnish the exposed edges, punch the stitch holes with a pricking iron or awl, saddle stitch the side and base seams by hand, and finish the stitch ends.
preFix.sourceNotes: The Leatherworking Handbook, Valerie Michael. Tandy Leather craft guides. Al Stohlman leathercraft series.

content.text: A two-panel leather phone sleeve: back panel, front panel, stitched at the sides and base: is the entry point for leatherworking. The materials cost is low and the technique covers the core skills: accurate cutting, edge preparation, hole punching, and
saddle stitch
. Use 1 to 1.5 mm
vegetable-tanned leather
: thicker leather makes the sleeve too rigid, thinner leather does not hold its shape.
Step 1, make a paper template

Measure the phone: height, width, and thickness. Add 5 mm to the height (for ease of removal), 3 mm to the width (clearance), and no addition for thickness: the leather will flex around the sides. The front panel is cut to these dimensions. The back panel is cut 5 mm taller to form a small lip above the front panel. Cut two panels from card and test the fit around the phone before cutting leather.
Step 2, cut the leather panels

Place the paper template on the flesh side of the leather and mark around it with the pencil. Cut along the marked lines with the leatherwork knife held vertical, making one clean stroke rather than sawing. Place the leather on the cutting mat. For straight edges, guide the knife against a metal ruler. Both panels must be cut with the grain running in the same direction.
Step 3, bevel and burnish the top edges
On the top edge of both panels (the open edge of the sleeve), run the
edge beveller
along both the flesh side and grain side of the cut edge to remove the sharp corner. Dampen the edge with a small amount of water on a finger. Rub the
leather burnisher

rapidly back and forth along the dampened edge until the fibres compress and the edge takes on a hard, smooth finish. The side and base edges are not burnished at this stage: they will be stitched together.
Step 4, punch the stitch holes
Place both panels together, grain-side out, aligning all edges. Mark the stitch line 3 mm from the edge on both side and base edges with the wing dividers or combination square. Using a
pricking iron
and mallet, punch the stitch holes through both panels together along the stitch line. Hold the iron vertical. Overlap the last hole of each set to maintain even spacing.
Step 5, saddle stitch the seams

Thread two needles on one length of waxed thread five times the length of the seam. Pass needle one through the first hole from front to back. Even up the thread. Pass needle one through the second hole from front to back, then pass needle two through the same hole from back to front, crossing over needle one's thread inside the hole. Pull both needles to tighten. Continue for all holes. At the last hole, backstitch through the previous two holes to secure, then trim the thread close to the surface.
Variations

To add a magnetic clasp, set a magnetic rivet through the front panel before stitching. Position it so the magnet lines up with the phone's power button area. To add a card pocket, cut a smaller panel from thinner leather and stitch it to the inside face of the back panel before assembling the sleeve.

attrs.fix: Use binder clips at the unpunched end of the seam to hold both panels in alignment while stitching. Always punch both panels as one unit.
attrs.cause: Holes not punched through both panels together; panels shifted relative to each other during stitching.
attrs.symptom: Stitching pulls the leather panels out of alignment

attrs.fix: Use a heavy linen or polyester thread pulled through a wax block. The thread should fill the hole but not require extreme force to pass through.
attrs.cause: Thread not waxed; thread too thin for the hole size; pulling too hard.

attrs.fix: After stitching, use the edge beveller on all stitched edges to remove any remaining sharp corner, then dampen and burnish to a smooth finish.
attrs.cause: Panels not cut cleanly; edges not bevelled and burnished after stitching.
attrs.symptom: Edges of the leather sleeve look rough and ragged after stitching

initialFindings.message: PATTERN tutorial has no Method/Steps section and no orderedList — sequential instructions missing
tricolon: tricolon (three parallel items) — two adjectives almost always beats three
tricolon: tricolon (three parallel items) — two adjectives almost always beats three
